content_en:
Al-Jarh wa al-Tadil (narrator criticism, literally disparagement and accreditation) is the science of evaluating the reliability of hadith transmitters, and Al-Kifayah fi Ilm al-Riwayah contains one of the fullest early treatments of its principles and procedures. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi approaches this subject with the understanding that it involves speaking about individuals, and he therefore begins by establishing its religious legitimacy.

The Permissibility of Narrator Criticism

Al-Khatib cites Quranic verses and hadiths that establish the necessity of verifying reports and rejecting the word of morally unreliable individuals. He quotes the Prophet's injunction to verify news from unknown sources and the Companions' practice of asking about narrators' characters before accepting their transmissions. He presents narrator criticism not as backbiting (ghibah) — which is forbidden — but as a form of legitimate counsel (nasiha) for the protection of the religion.

He notes the consensus of major scholars — including Ahmad ibn Hanbal, Yahya ibn Maeen, and al-Bukhari himself — that criticizing unreliable narrators is obligatory when necessary to protect the integrity of hadith, and that a scholar who knows a narrator's weakness and conceals it out of false piety bears responsibility for any harm that follows from the acceptance of unreliable reports.

Levels of Praise

Al-Khatib presents a graded vocabulary of commendation, from the highest praise to qualified approval. Terms like thiqah thiqah (absolutely trustworthy), thiqah thabt (trustworthy and firm), hafiz (precise memorizer), and mutqin (sound) indicate the highest levels of reliability. Lower grades include la ba's bihi (no objection to him), shaykh (a teacher, implying acceptability without enthusiasm), and yuktab hadithuh (his hadiths are written down, meaning they may be recorded for evaluation but are not a primary source).

He emphasizes that the vocabulary of commendation was not fixed across all scholars and that the same term could carry different weights in different scholars' usage. A student of hadith must therefore understand the individual idiom of each major critic — knowing that Yahya ibn Maeen's la ba's bihi was higher than al-Nasa'i's similar-sounding phrase, for example.

Levels of Disparagement

The vocabulary of criticism is correspondingly graded, from mild to severe. Layyin al-hadith (soft in hadith) or fihi maqal (there is something to say about him) indicates slight weakness. Da'if (weak) means the narrator's hadiths require corroboration. Munkar al-hadith (rejected in hadith) or matruk (abandoned) indicates serious unreliability. The most severe terms — kadhdhab (liar), wada' al-hadith (fabricator of hadith) — indicate that the narrator's reports are not merely weak but actively dangerous and should be discarded entirely.

Disputed Cases and Scholarly Disagreement

Al-Khatib devotes considerable attention to the many narrators about whom scholars disagreed. He presents the methodological principle that disparagement (jarh) generally takes precedence over accreditation (tadil) when the grounds for criticism are specified, because the critic knows something the accreditor may have missed. However, when accreditation is overwhelming and criticism is vague or unspecified, the accreditation may be preferred.

He also addresses the question of whether criticism from a contemporary or rival of the narrator should be weighted differently from that of a neutral observer. Al-Khatib's balanced treatment of these issues established the framework within which subsequent hadith scholars — Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani in Tahdhib al-Tahdhib and al-Dhahabi in Mizan al-Itidal — would evaluate the thousands of narrators who appear in the isnad literature.
content_id:
Al-Jarh wa al-Ta'dil (kritik perawi — secara harfiah: celaan dan pengakuan/pujian) adalah ilmu yang mengevaluasi keandalan para perawi hadis, dan Al-Kifayah fi Ilm al-Riwayah memuat salah satu pembahasan paling lengkap dan awal tentang prinsip-prinsip dan metodologinya. Bagi Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, tanpa ilmu jarh wa ta'dil, mustahil untuk menentukan mana hadis yang sahih dan mana yang dha'if atau maudhu'.

Al-Khatib mendiskusikan siapa yang memiliki wewenang untuk mengkritik atau memuji perawi, apa kriteria yang digunakan dalam penilaian tersebut, dan bagaimana menyelesaikan pertentangan antara pujian dan celaan terhadap perawi yang sama. Beliau menjelaskan bahwa para ulama jarh wa ta'dil menggunakan tingkatan-tingkatan tertentu dalam mengungkapkan penilaian mereka, mulai dari tingkatan paling tinggi dalam pujian hingga tingkatan paling rendah dalam celaan.

Salah satu prinsip penting yang dibahas adalah kaidah "al-jarh muqaddam 'ala al-ta'dil" (celaan didahulukan atas pujian). Namun Al-Khatib menjelaskan bahwa penerapan kaidah ini tidak bersifat mutlak — ia bergantung pada sejumlah kondisi, termasuk apakah celaan tersebut disertai alasan yang jelas (mufassar) atau tidak (mubham).

Al-Khatib juga membahas persoalan apakah seorang ulama boleh mengkritik perawi secara bersamaan dengan memujinya dalam aspek-aspek yang berbeda, bagaimana menilai perawi yang hanya dikenal oleh sebagian kecil ulama, dan bagaimana memperlakukan riwayat perawi yang terkenal berbuat bid'ah tetapi tidak berdusta dalam hadis.

Pembahasan ilmu jarh wa ta'dil dalam Al-Kifayah menjadi acuan penting bagi generasi-generasi ulama berikutnya dalam menerapkan kriteria penilaian yang adil dan metodis terhadap para perawi hadis.
content_ar: Al-Jarh wa al-Tadil (narrator criticism, literally disparagement and accreditation) is the science of evaluating the reliability of الحديث transmitters, and Al-Kifayah fi Ilm al-Riwayah contains one of the fullest early treatments of its principles and procedures. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi approaches this subject with the الفهم that it involves speaking about individuals, and he لذلك begins by establishing its religious legitimacy. The Permissibility of Narrator Criticism Al-Khatib cites القرآني الآيات and hadiths that establish the necessity of verifying reports and rejecting the word of morally unreliable individuals. He quotes النبي ﷺ's injunction to verify news from unknown sources and the الصحابة' practice of asking about narrators' characters قبل accepting their transmissions. He presents narrator criticism not as backbiting ( ghibah ) — which is forbidden — but as a form of legitimate counsel ( nasiha ) for the protection of the religion. He notes the consensus of major العلماء — بما في ذلك أحمد ابن حنبل, Yahya ibn Maeen, and البخاري himself — that criticizing unreliable narrators is obligatory عندما necessary to protect the integrity of الحديث, and that a scholar who knows a narrator's الضعف and conceals it out of false piety bears المسؤولية for any harm that follows from the acceptance of unreliable reports. Levels of Praise Al-Khatib presents a graded vocabulary of commendation, from the highest praise to qualified approval. Terms like thiqah thiqah (absolutely trustworthy), thiqah thabt (trustworthy and firm), hafiz (precise memorizer), and mutqin (sound) indicate the highest levels of reliability. Lower grades include la ba's bihi (no objection to him), shaykh (a teacher, implying acceptability without enthusiasm), and yuktab hadithuh (his hadiths are written down, meaning they may be recorded for evaluation but are not a الأساسي source). He emphasizes that the vocabulary of commendation was not fixed across all العلماء and that the same term could carry different weights in different العلماء' usage. A student of الحديث must لذلك understand the individual idiom of each major critic — knowing that Yahya ibn Maeen's la ba's bihi was higher than al-النسائي's similar-sounding phrase, على سبيل المثال. Levels of Disparagement The vocabulary of criticism is correspondingly graded, from mild to severe. Layyin al-الحديث (soft in الحديث) or fihi maqal (there is something to say about him) indicates slight الضعف. ضعيف (weak) means the narrator's hadiths require corroboration. Munkar al-الحديث (rejected in الحديث) or matruk (abandoned) indicates serious unreliability. The most severe terms — kadhdhab (liar), wada' al-الحديث (fabricator of الحديث) — indicate that
